docs.title: Rifleman
docs.tldr: The Marine Corps rifleman is the ground-combat backbone of the USMC infantry — small-unit leader, marksman, and the Marine everything else supports.
docs.body: The rifleman is the Marine the rest of the Corps exists to support. You close with the enemy on foot, lead fire teams, and become an expert with the service rifle. This is ground combat — small units, long days, and the discipline that comes from doing the same hard things until they're automatic. Training pipeline runs through Parris Island or San Diego, then School of Infantry East or West. Most 0311s end up in an infantry battalion at Camp Pendleton, Camp Lejeune, or Kaneohe Bay. Expect deployments. Infantry 03 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Infantry Training School of Infantry (East or West) Camp Geiger, NC or Camp Pendleton, CA Law Enforcement Officer Public Safety Security Officer / Supervisor Security Services Federal Protective Service / Federal Air Marshal Federal Law Enforcement Do I have to start as 0311, or can I enlist open-contract? You can enlist with an infantry contract specifically (03xx field) and be classified to 0311 or another infantry MOS at SOI based on performance and needs of the Marine Corps. Some ship with a guaranteed MOS contract. Talk to your recruiter about the difference between open-contract, field-guaranteed, and MOS-guaranteed options. What's the contract length? Standard active-duty infantry contracts are 4 years active. Some options include an additional IRR (Inactive Ready Reserve) commitment on top, extending the total service obligation. Can women enlist as 0311? Yes. All ground combat MOSs opened to qualified women following the 2013-2015 DoD policy changes and subsequent Marine Corps integration. Standards are the same regardless of gender. What happens if I get injured during training? Training injuries are common at SOI. Depending on severity, you may be rolled to the next class, transferred to medical hold, or medically separated. Verify current Marine Corps injury-return-to-duty policy through your chain of command. Can I go MARSOC from 0311? Yes. MARSOC Assessment & Selection is open to active-duty Marines from most MOSs including 0311. You attend A&S after submitting a package; selection is followed by the Individual Training Course (ITC). Attrition is high. See 0372 Critical Skills Operator entry for details. Lee Marvin R. Lee Ermey Dakota Meyer John Basilone Kyle Carpenter Drew Carey

docs.title: Scout Sniper
docs.tldr: Marine Corps long-range reconnaissance and precision-fire specialist — status under review in recent Force Design changes.
docs.body: Scout snipers are the infantry's long-range eyes and precision-fire specialists. You earn this MOS by passing a selection course and Scout Sniper Basic Course — the washout rate is high and the training is hard on purpose. Status and force structure of this MOS have shifted with Force Design; confirm current standing before relying on anything here. Infantry 03 Prerequisite: 0311 qualified See 0311 entry Various Scout Sniper Basic Course SSBC Camp Lejeune, NC or Camp Pendleton, CA Law Enforcement Tactical Officer (SWAT) Public Safety Carlos Hathcock Chuck Mawhinney

docs.title: Machine Gunner
docs.tldr: Marine infantry machine gunner — crew-served weapons specialist providing suppressive and sustained fire.
docs.body: You run the platoon's heavy and medium machine guns. Think M240, M2 .50 cal, and the support-by-fire position in almost every infantry maneuver. Expect to carry weight and to be the first thing the enemy wants to silence. Infantry 03 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Infantry Training School of Infantry Camp Geiger, NC or Camp Pendleton, CA Law Enforcement Officer Public Safety

docs.title: Infantry Unit Leader
docs.tldr: Senior enlisted Marine Corps infantry leader — platoon sergeant and above; the promotion progression for career infantrymen.
docs.body: The 0369 is the senior enlisted infantry leader — platoon sergeant, company gunnery sergeant, battalion-level billets. You get here by staying in, leading well, and proving you can run complex infantry operations at the platoon and company level. Infantry 03 Infantry Unit Leaders Course IULC Camp Lejeune, NC or Camp Pendleton, CA Operations Manager Logistics / Operations

docs.title: Radio Operator
docs.tldr: Marine Corps tactical radio operator — builds and runs voice and data networks for operational units.
docs.body: Comms Marines. You set up and run the radio networks that infantry, logistics, and command elements depend on. Heavy emphasis on HF, VHF, UHF, and tactical satellite. Expect to be attached to infantry units as often as you're with a comms unit. Communications 06 Recruit Training MCRD Parris Island or San Diego Basic Radio Operator Course Marine Corps Communication-Electronics School Twentynine Palms, CA Telecommunications Technician Telecommunications Network Support Specialist Information Technology

docs.title: Field Artillery Cannoneer
docs.tldr: Marine Corps artillery crew member — operates howitzers to provide indirect fire support for ground combat.
docs.body: Artillery cannoneers man the M777 howitzer and related fire-support systems. You're part of a gun crew that puts rounds downrange in support of maneuver units. Physical job with crew drills you'll run thousands of times. Field Artillery 08 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Field Artillery Cannoneer Course Fort Sill (joint with Army) Fort Sill, OK Heavy Equipment Operator Construction

docs.title: Military Police
docs.tldr: Marine Corps military police — base law enforcement, force protection, and deployed security operations.
docs.body: Marine MP. You work base security, traffic enforcement, criminal investigation at the entry level, and can deploy for detainee operations or force protection. Strong translation to civilian law enforcement. Military Police & Corrections 58 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Military Police Basic Course USMC Military Police Basic Course Fort Leonard Wood, MO Police Officer Public Safety Corrections Officer Public Safety Private Security Supervisor Security

docs.title: Motor Vehicle Operator
docs.tldr: Marine Corps motor vehicle operator — drives tactical and support vehicles; strong civilian CDL translation.
docs.body: You drive the Marine Corps. MRAPs, 7-tons, MTVRs, HMMWVs, buses — convoy ops, logistics runs, troop movement. The skills transfer directly to a CDL in the civilian world, and the Marine Corps typically pays for the test through SkillBridge or DoD COOL. Motor Transport 35 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Motor Transport Operator Course Marine Corps Combat Service Support Schools Camp Johnson, NC (Camp Lejeune) Commercial Truck Driver (CDL) Trucking / Logistics Bus Driver Transit

docs.title: Intelligence Specialist
docs.tldr: Marine Corps all-source intelligence analyst — supports tactical and operational commanders with intel products.
docs.body: Intelligence analyst at the tactical and operational level. You produce intel products, analyze enemy capabilities, and brief commanders. Desk-heavy compared to combat arms but you deploy with infantry and MARSOC units as the intel support element. Intelligence 02 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Intelligence Basic Course Navy Marine Corps Intelligence Training Center Virginia Beach, VA (Dam Neck) Intelligence Analyst (Federal / Contractor) Government / Defense Business Intelligence Analyst Corporate

docs.title: Aviation Supply Specialist
docs.tldr: Marine Corps aviation supply specialist — manages parts and consumables for Marine aircraft squadrons.
docs.body: You manage aviation parts and supply for a Marine aircraft wing. Inventory systems, parts ordering, receipt and issue, and the administrative side of keeping jets and helos flying. Office environment with squadron and hangar-deck work. Aviation Logistics 66 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Aviation Supply Specialist Course Navy supply school (joint training) NAS Meridian, MS or Navy Supply Corps School Logistician Aviation / Logistics Supply Chain Analyst Corporate Logistics

docs.title: Mortarman
docs.tldr: Marine infantry mortarman — 60mm and 81mm indirect-fire specialist supporting maneuver units.
docs.body: Mortar crewman. You run 60mm and 81mm mortars providing indirect fire to the rifle squads and platoons. Heavy tubes, heavy plates, lots of math on the fire-direction side. Arguably one of the most physically demanding jobs in the infantry. Infantry 03 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Infantry Training School of Infantry Camp Geiger, NC or Camp Pendleton, CA Law Enforcement Officer Public Safety

docs.title: Antitank Missileman
docs.tldr: Marine infantry antitank specialist — operates TOW, Javelin, and other antitank missile systems.
docs.body: Antitank. You operate the TOW missile system, Javelin, and other antitank weapons. Combines infantry fieldcraft with weapons-system training. Verify current T/O — antitank structure has shifted under Marine Corps Force Design. Infantry 03 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Infantry Training School of Infantry Camp Geiger, NC or Camp Pendleton, CA Law Enforcement Officer Public Safety

docs.title: Reconnaissance Marine
docs.tldr: Marine Corps ground reconnaissance — tier-advanced infantry with amphibious, airborne, and direct-action training.
docs.body: Recon Marines conduct ground reconnaissance, amphibious reconnaissance, and direct-action operations in support of MAGTF operations. Pipeline is brutal — RTAP, Basic Recon Course, and follow-on schools (jump, dive, survival). One of the most demanding non-MARSOC pipelines in the Marine Corps. Infantry 03 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Infantry Training School of Infantry Camp Geiger, NC or Camp Pendleton, CA Recon Training Assessment Program (RTAP) RTAP Camp Pendleton, CA Basic Reconnaissance Course (BRC) BRC Camp Pendleton, CA Federal Tactical Officer (FBI HRT, DEA FAST, USMS SOG) Federal Law Enforcement Private Maritime / High-Risk Security Security Col. William 'Rich' Higgins Jason Everman

docs.title: Infantry Officer
docs.tldr: Marine Corps infantry officer — leads rifle platoons and companies in ground combat operations.
docs.body: The infantry officer leads Marines in ground combat. You commission as a 2nd Lieutenant, run The Basic School with every other new Marine officer, then attend Infantry Officer Course (IOC — the hardest initial officer pipeline in the Marine Corps). From there you lead a rifle platoon, then a weapons platoon, and by Captain you command a company. Infantry 03 Commissioning source OCS / NROTC / USNA / PLC / ECP Various The Basic School (TBS) Marine Corps Officer Basic School Quantico, VA Infantry Officer Course (IOC) IOC Quantico, VA Operations Manager Corporate Project Manager (PMP) Cross-industry

docs.title: Infantry Weapons Officer (Marine Gunner)
docs.tldr: Marine Corps infantry weapons warrant officer — the Marine Gunner; selected from senior enlisted infantry.
docs.body: The Marine Gunner is the infantry weapons subject-matter expert at the battalion and regimental level. You select as a Chief Warrant Officer from the senior enlisted infantry ranks (typically GySgt or MSgt). You wear a distinctive bursting-bomb insignia on your collar and you're the go-to expert on every weapons system the infantry uses. Infantry 03 Senior enlisted infantry experience Various infantry units Fleet assignment Warrant Officer Basic Course The Basic School — WO track Quantico, VA Infantry Weapons Officer Course Marine Gunner Course Quantico, VA Technical Expert / Subject-Matter Expert Defense / Consulting

docs.title: Combat Engineer
docs.tldr: Marine Corps combat engineer — demolition, breaching, obstacle reduction, and combat construction.
docs.body: Combat engineers clear obstacles, breach fortifications, emplace demolitions, and build defensive positions. You operate alongside the infantry but carry the engineering expertise — mines, explosives, mobility and counter-mobility tasks. Attached to infantry units for most deployments. Engineer, Construction, Equipment, and Shore Party 13 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Combat Engineer Course Marine Corps Engineer School Courthouse Bay, Camp Lejeune, NC Demolitions / Blasting Technician Construction / Mining Construction Equipment Operator Construction

docs.title: Aircraft Ordnance Technician
docs.tldr: Marine Corps aviation ordnance technician — loads and maintains weapons systems on Marine aircraft.
docs.body: Aviation ordnance Marines load bombs, missiles, and guns on Marine aircraft — F-35, F/A-18, AH-1, and others. Loudest job on the flight line. Safety-critical work with a tight feedback loop: something's wrong, you hear it from the pilot, fast. Aviation Ordnance 65 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Aircraft Ordnance Course Center for Naval Aviation Technical Training NAS Pensacola, FL or NAS Oceana, VA Aircraft Mechanic (powerplant / airframe) Aviation

docs.title: Parachute Rigger (Air Delivery Specialist)
docs.tldr: Marine Corps parachute rigger — packs parachutes, rigs aerial delivery, jump-qualified logistics specialist.
docs.body: Air delivery specialists pack parachutes, rig cargo for aerial delivery, and are among the few Marines whose primary MOS makes them jump-qualified. Small community, specialized skill set. Supports Marine airborne operations and aerial resupply. Logistics 04 Recruit Training Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, SC or San Diego, CA Basic Airborne Course Army Airborne School Fort Moore, GA Parachute Rigger Course Army Rigger School Fort Gregg-Adams, VA FAA Senior Parachute Rigger Aviation

docs.title: Infantryman
docs.tldr: Army infantryman — close-combat ground fighter across light, airborne, mechanized, and Stryker formations.
docs.body: The Army's infantry backbone. Light, airborne, mountain, Stryker, or mechanized — the unit type shapes the day-to-day but the core job is close-combat ground fighting. Training runs through One Station Unit Training at Fort Moore (formerly Fort Benning). Infantry 11 One Station Unit Training (OSUT) Infantry OSUT Fort Moore, GA Law Enforcement Officer Public Safety Corrections Officer Public Safety Federal Law Enforcement (CBP, ICE, USMS) Federal Law Enforcement What's the difference between 11B and 11C? 11B is the rifleman MOS — the base of the infantry squad. 11C is Indirect Fire Infantryman — runs mortars. Both are in the 11-series and you can lateral between them with additional training, but they specialize differently from OSUT onward. How long is OSUT for 11B? One Station Unit Training combines Basic Combat Training and Advanced Individual Training for infantry soldiers in one continuous course. Historically 14 weeks; extended in recent years to around 22 weeks. Verify current length before enlisting. Can I pick my unit type (airborne, Ranger, light, mech)? You can volunteer for Airborne School as an Option 4 contract. Ranger School comes later — typically after arrival at unit and meeting qualifications. Light/mech/Stryker assignment depends on the needs of the Army and your first duty station, not necessarily your preference. What's the deployment tempo like now? Varies by unit and Army posture. Historically 9-12 month deployments every 18-24 months. Recent years have shifted toward shorter rotations or security-cooperation deployments. Your unit's patch pattern is a good tell — check what they've deployed to in the last five years. Audie Murphy Sammy L. Davis Dakota Meyer Alvin York

docs.title: Combat Medic Specialist
docs.tldr: Army combat medic — trauma care, troop medical clinic work, and first-responder for infantry and aviation units.
docs.body: The Army's enlisted medic. You train in trauma care at Fort Sam Houston, attach to an infantry squad or aviation unit, and are the first responder for everything from training injuries to combat casualties. One of the few MOSs where civilian translation is immediate — NREMT-certified on graduation. Medical 68 Basic Combat Training BCT Fort Jackson, SC / Fort Moore, GA / Fort Sill, OK / Fort Leonard Wood, MO 68W AIT Medical Education & Training Campus (METC) Fort Sam Houston, TX Emergency Medical Technician (EMT) Healthcare Paramedic Healthcare Registered Nurse (via bridge program) Healthcare

docs.title: Cyber Defense Operations
docs.tldr: Air Force defensive cyber operator — monitors, hunts, and responds to threats on Air Force networks.
docs.body: Defensive cyber for the Air Force. You run intrusion detection, hunt threats on Air Force networks, and respond to incidents. Classroom-heavy pipeline, fast-evolving skill set, and the clearance you earn is a career asset for decades after you separate. Cyberspace Operations 1D Basic Military Training BMT Joint Base San Antonio-Lackland, TX Cyber Technical Training Keesler AFB Keesler AFB, MS Information Security Analyst Information Technology Cybersecurity Analyst (SOC / IR) Information Technology Penetration Tester Information Technology How long is the tech school? Keesler cyber tech training runs approximately 6-9 months of block instruction depending on the specific pipeline and current course length. Verify current length in the AFECD. What clearance do I need? Top Secret (TS) minimum. Many billets require TS/SCI. The background investigation starts during tech school and typically takes 6-18 months; you may be assigned interim access during processing. Can I take DoD COOL certs on active duty? Yes. DoD COOL funds CompTIA (Security+, CySA+, CASP+), ISACA (CISM), (ISC)² (CISSP), and others. Many airmen stack 3-5 certs during a 4-year term. Does the Air Force let me work remote cyber? Limited. Classified networks require in-person work at a cleared facility. Unclassified work can occasionally be remote, but the default is on-site. General Timothy D. Haugh

docs.title: Tactical Air Control Party (TACP)
docs.tldr: Air Force Tactical Air Control Party — embeds with ground units to call in and coordinate close air support.
docs.body: TACP. Air Force ground warfare. You embed with Army or Marine infantry units and call in close air support when they're in a fight. High-attrition pipeline, infantry-adjacent physical standards, and a tight community. Often conflated with Combat Controllers (different AFSC, even harder pipeline) — TACP is the most common ground-embed AFSC. Command and Control 1C BMT Basic Military Training JBSA-Lackland, TX TACP Apprentice Course TACP Schoolhouse JBSA-Lackland, TX Joint Terminal Attack Controller (JTAC) qualification JTAC QC Various Federal Tactical Officer Public Safety

docs.title: Boatswain's Mate
docs.tldr: U.S. Navy deck seaman — runs shipboard deck operations including underway replenishment, anchoring, and small-boat handling.
docs.body: If a ship is a city, the BM is the infrastructure crew. You run deck evolutions: underway replenishments, anchor detail, small-boat ops, line handling during pier work. Physical job, lots of time outdoors, and you earn surface warfare qualifications as you advance. Traditional rating with a long history in the fleet. Deck BM Recruit Training Recruit Training Command Great Lakes, IL Apprenticeship / A-School Boatswain's Mate A-School or direct-to-fleet Great Lakes, IL (A-School) or assigned ship Merchant Mariner / Able Seaman Maritime Dockworker / Stevedore Maritime Logistics

docs.title: Hospital Corpsman
docs.tldr: Navy medical rating — hospital and clinic care, plus the Marine Corps FMF combat-medic pipeline. One of the most specialty-diverse ratings in the military.
docs.body: Navy medics. Dual-track rating — some HMs stay in hospitals and clinics, others attach to the Marine Corps (FMF) and deploy with infantry battalions. The FMF side is closer to combat medicine than any other non-Army pathway. NECs layer specialties on top: advanced trauma, dental, behavioral health, SARC (SEAL medic), and dozens more. Medical HM Recruit Training Recruit Training Command Great Lakes, IL Hospital Corpsman A-School Basic Hospital Corpsman School Fort Sam Houston, TX (METC) Field Medical Training (FMF path only) Field Medical Training Battalion Camp Pendleton, CA or Camp Lejeune, NC Emergency Medical Technician / Paramedic Healthcare Registered Nurse (via bridge program) Healthcare Physician Assistant (via IPAP) Healthcare Hospital corpsman vs. FMF corpsman — can I pick? You can indicate preference. Some corpsmen start hospital-side and request FMTB later; others ship straight to FMTB. Not everyone gets FMF duty — it depends on orders and needs of the Navy. Will I be a 'Doc' to Marines? Yes. Corpsmen attached to Marine units are called 'Doc' regardless of their actual rank. Deep tradition between Marines and their corpsmen. Can I become a civilian nurse or PA? Yes — HM is one of the strongest civilian-transition ratings. NREMT is often earned during training. RN bridge programs accept HM service credit. IPAP lets you commission as a PA officer in-service. Donald E. Ballard Richard D. Dewert

docs.title: Maritime Enforcement Specialist
docs.tldr: U.S. Coast Guard law-enforcement rating — boardings, maritime security, counter-drug, and port protection.
docs.body: Coast Guard law enforcement. You run boardings, enforce maritime law, support counter-drug operations, and do port security. Strong civilian translation to federal, state, and local law enforcement — CG MEs are often recruited heavily by Border Patrol and other federal agencies. Law Enforcement / Maritime Security ME Recruit Training Coast Guard Training Center Cape May, NJ ME A-School Maritime Enforcement Specialist School Charleston, SC (FLETC) Federal Law Enforcement Officer (CBP, HSI, DEA) Federal Law Enforcement Police Officer Public Safety Do CG MEs deploy? Cutter-based MEs deploy on Coast Guard patrols (weeks to months at sea). Some MEs deploy in joint operations with DoD. Not the same deployment tempo as Navy/Marine/Army. Is FLETC certification automatic? You complete portions of FLETC training as part of ME A-School; full federal law-enforcement officer certification typically requires additional training or hire by a federal agency. Douglas Munro

docs.title: Aviation Survival Technician (Rescue Swimmer)
docs.tldr: U.S. Coast Guard Aviation Survival Technician — the rescue swimmer; deploys from helicopters to recover people from the water.
docs.body: Coast Guard rescue swimmers. ASTs jump out of helicopters into whatever the Atlantic, Pacific, or Gulf of Mexico is doing that day to pull people out of the water. Brutal selection pipeline — one of the highest attrition rates in the U.S. military — and one of the most immediately meaningful missions in the service. Aviation / Rescue AST Recruit Training CGTC Cape May Cape May, NJ Airman Program (apprenticeship) Assigned Air Station Various AST A-School Aviation Survival Technician School USCG Training Center Elizabeth City, NC Paramedic Healthcare Rescue Technician (fire/HAZMAT) Public Safety

docs.title: Cyberspace Operations
docs.tldr: Space Force cyberspace operator — cyber defense and offensive operations in support of space missions.
docs.body: Cyber for the Space Force. Closer to Air Force 1D7X1 than any pure space operations role — Space Force inherited its cyber structure from the Air Force and is still evolving its own standards. Expect to work alongside Air Force cyber counterparts early in your career. Cyberspace Operations 5C Basic Military Training BMT (joint with Air Force) Joint Base San Antonio-Lackland, TX Cyber Technical Training Keesler AFB Keesler AFB, MS Information Security Analyst Information Technology Cybersecurity Engineer Information Technology Is Space Force really separate from the Air Force? Yes — Space Force is an independent service under the Department of the Air Force. It has its own ranks (Specialist 1-4, Sergeant, Technical Sergeant, Master Sergeant, Chief Master Sergeant) and classification system. Many Space Force members transferred from the Air Force in 2020-2021. Do Space Force members go to space? No — Space Force operates space-based systems from Earth. Astronauts belong to NASA. Space Force handles satellites, ground control, and cyber defense for space missions. Chief Master Sergeant of the Space Force Roger A. Towberman

docs.body: MOS jobs in the U.S. Marine Corps

docs.title: U.S. Army
docs.body: MOS jobs in the U.S. Army

docs.body: AFSC jobs in the U.S. Air Force

docs.title: How to read a job entry on EveryMOS
docs.tldr: Every page on this site follows the same structure. Here's a walk-through of what each section means and how to use it.
docs.body:
Every page on this site follows the same structure. Here's a walk-through of what each section means and how to use it. Tools I designed the job pages to answer the questions I would have asked at 18 — if I could have gotten a straight answer.

Here's what each section means, and how to read it without assuming anything you shouldn't.

## The header

Three things matter: the code (mono font, branch-accented), the title, and the **confidence badge**. If it says "uncertain" or "stub," the entry is a draft and I haven't finished verifying it. The TL;DR below the header is one sentence — the shortest honest description of the job.

## Requirements

ASVAB is displayed two ways: the **raw requirement** (what the branch publishes, exactly) and the structured composites. The raw string is the source of truth; the composites are there so I can eventually build a "what jobs do I qualify for" tool.

Clearance levels range from **none** to **TS/SCI**. Higher clearance = longer pipeline, more paperwork, and a more valuable record when you separate.

## Training pipeline

This is the path from enlistment to MOS-qualified. Each step shows the school, location, and how many weeks. The total at the top is the honest "how long until I'm doing the job" number.

## Career roadmap

This is the section most sites skip and the one that matters most.

**Standard progression** shows rank-by-rank what you'll typically be doing. The typical years-in-service ranges are illustrative — real promotion depends on cutting scores, zones, and billet availability, which shift year to year.

**B-billets** are special-duty tours. Recruiter, drill instructor, Marine Security Guard, SOI instructor. They're typically three years, they put you outside your operational unit, and they can accelerate promotion if you do well.

**Lateral moves** are other jobs in the same occupational field. Some are "walk in and request it"; some require selection courses with high washout rates. I note which is which.

**Reclassification** is how you leave the MOS entirely. Usually gated on re-enlistment and classification approval, and not guaranteed — the Marine Corps has to need you in the new MOS.

## Civilian crosswalk

Civilian jobs that translate. O*NET codes link to Department of Labor data. Transferable certifications are what you can earn through DoD COOL while you're in, which translates directly to resumes on the outside.

## Sources

Every entry cites its sources. Official branch sites (`.mil`) beat classification manuals; classification manuals beat third-party sites. If I couldn't verify something, the confidence drops and it goes in the notes.

---

That's the whole structure. Read the confidence badge first, the career roadmap second, the civilian crosswalk third. Everything else is reference.

docs.title: Why 0311 is the Marine Corps' ground-combat backbone
docs.tldr: The rifleman is the Marine everything else supports. Here's what that actually means once you get to the fleet.
docs.body:
The rifleman is the Marine everything else supports. Here's what that actually means once you get to the fleet. Jobs Deep-Dive Every Marine is a rifleman. It's a cliché, and also true. But the MOS 0311 — the actual rifleman — sits at the center of Marine Corps combat operations in a way no other specialty does.

## What the job actually is

As an 0311 you run a fire team, a squad, or eventually a platoon. Your day-to-day is training to fight on foot — patrolling, room clearing, fire-and-maneuver drills, live-fire exercises, and the endless logistics of getting a small unit from point A to point B ready to shoot.

On a pump (deployment), the rhythm shifts to actual operations. In combat, the rifleman closes with the enemy. In peacetime training rotations — MEU floats, MWX at Twentynine Palms, Philippines deployments — you're rehearsing the same thing.

## What makes the job hard

Weight. Distance. Weather. Boredom punctuated by intensity. Long days when you're cold or tired and still have to perform. The best 0311s I knew weren't the biggest or the fastest — they were the ones who could keep making decent decisions at hour 40.

The culture is direct. If you're slow or lazy or unclear, someone's going to tell you. That's not cruelty — it's how small-unit leadership corrects itself in real time. You get used to it.

## The career arc

Enlist. Boot camp. School of Infantry. First unit assignment. You'll be a fire team member for a year or two, then a fire team leader once you're a Corporal. By E-5 you're a squad leader running 12–13 Marines. By E-6 you're a platoon sergeant, and at that point the career-infantry track laterals you to 0369.

In between, the B-billets: recruiter, drill instructor, Marine Security Guard at an embassy. Three years each. Hard work, but they accelerate promotion and give you perspective outside the infantry.

Some 0311s lateral to 0321 Recon (high bar — BRC has brutal attrition) or to 0317 Scout Sniper (status currently under review as part of Force Design). A smaller number try MARSOC. The rest stay in the infantry and spend a career getting better at one hard thing.

## What it's worth afterward

Civilian translation for 0311 is honest: law enforcement, security, federal agencies. The discipline transfers. The leadership transfers. The specific technical skills (M16, M240, M320) don't transfer directly — but the proof that you can lead small teams under pressure does, and that's what you sell.

DoD COOL doesn't fund a lot for 0311 directly. The bigger lever is the Post-9/11 GI Bill and SkillBridge in your last six months.

## What I'd tell someone considering it

If you want technical skills that make you rich on the outside, 0311 is the wrong MOS. If you want to prove to yourself that you can do a hard thing with a small team and carry that forward, it's the right one.

There's no third reason.

docs.title: The four ASVAB mistakes almost everyone makes
docs.tldr: The test is easier than the study materials make it look. Here's where time gets wasted.
docs.body:
The test is easier than the study materials make it look. Here's where time gets wasted. ASVAB The ASVAB is a timed, multiple-choice, adaptive-ish battery that decides what jobs you qualify for. Most of the people I know who bombed it didn't bomb the content — they bombed the prep.

## 1. Studying every section equally

The AFQT (the overall score) is computed from four subtests: Arithmetic Reasoning, Word Knowledge, Paragraph Comprehension, and Mathematics Knowledge. That's it. The other subtests (General Science, Auto and Shop Info, Mechanical Comprehension, Electronics, Assembling Objects) only matter for specific composite line scores.

If you don't have a target MOS that needs those line scores, ignore them during prep. Most 400-page study books are optimized for the wrong goal.

## 2. Not taking a practice test first

You should never start studying without knowing where you actually stand. Take a timed practice test the first day. It tells you which of the four AFQT subtests is your weakest — that's where the study hours should go.

## 3. Ignoring the math

Half of the AFQT is math. The math is not hard — it's middle-school and early high-school level — but the test doesn't give you a calculator, and the time pressure hits people who haven't done long division by hand since 8th grade.

Drill it. Twenty problems a day for two weeks closes most of the gap.

## 4. Skipping the follow-up if you score low

You can retake the ASVAB. First retake is a month after the first test; second is a month after the first retake; third is six months later. If your first score doesn't qualify you for the MOS you want, take the month and test again.

Also — already-serving Marines and soldiers can retake via the in-service testing program. Your ASVAB is not permanent.

---

The test is a gate, not a verdict. People who treat it like a gate pass it with the score they need.

docs.title: The quiet logic of the B-billet tour
docs.tldr: Recruiter, drill instructor, MSG, SOI instructor. Three years outside your unit. Here's why the career Marines pick them.
docs.body:
Recruiter, drill instructor, MSG, SOI instructor. Three years outside your unit. Here's why the career Marines pick them. Transition The Marine Corps calls them B-billets — special-duty assignments outside your operational unit. Recruiter, drill instructor, Marine Security Guard at an embassy, Combat Instructor at the School of Infantry. Three years each, sometimes extended. Every other service has its own version: Army drill sergeant, Navy RDC, Air Force MTI.

Most junior Marines hear "B-billet" and picture three years of screaming at recruits. That's the stereotype, and it's not wrong for DI duty. But B-billets are also a quiet career accelerator, and the career Marines know it.

## Why they help

**Promotion.** Marines with successful B-billet tours consistently advance faster than Marines who spend their entire career in operational units. Boards reward the evaluated leadership in a hard, high-visibility job. You get noticed. Your fitness reports run through a chain that actually reads them.

**Perspective.** You spend three years seeing the service from a different angle. Recruiters see the civilian side — what 18-year-olds think the Marine Corps is, what their parents are afraid of, what the local economy is doing. DIs see how Marines are made, which changes what you expect from your own junior Marines when you get back to the fleet. MSG duty means you run security at embassies and work alongside State Department and the intelligence community.

**Relationships.** You leave with a network outside your MOS community. Recruiters end up with contacts across every district. DIs cross paths with senior enlisted from every specialty. MSG stands you up alongside Marines from every MOS who also got picked.

## Why people avoid them

They're hard. Long hours. High standards. Constant scrutiny. You get moved to a new city for three years, often away from your operational community and friends. The workload on recruiter duty is famously brutal; DI duty is a full-time physical and psychological grind.

Also — B-billets interrupt your operational clock. If you're trying to deploy every two years or you love what your unit does, three years at a recruiting substation in Ohio feels like exile.

## How to pick

Line up your B-billet against your career goal.

- **If you want to make Gunnery Sergeant and beyond:** take the B-billet. Any of them. Make it count.
- **If you want to stay operational and lateral to something hard (recon, MARSOC, scout sniper):** go before the B-billet. You can B-billet after.
- **If you want to get out at 4 or 8:** skip. It only matters if you're staying in.
- **If you have family considerations (single parent, military spouse, chronically ill dependent):** talk honestly with your career counselor about which B-billet is workable. MSG with dependents is complicated; recruiter near family is usually doable.

## The pattern I saw

The Marines who ended up at E-8 and E-9 almost all had a B-billet on their record. Not because B-billet was magic — because it's a filter. The Corps is telling you it trusts you with a hard job outside your comfort zone, and the people who pass through it are the ones who get evaluated for the next hard job.

If you're planning on a 20-year career, the question isn't whether to do a B-billet. It's which one and when.

docs.title: Lateral moves vs. reclass: what actually happens when you want to change jobs
docs.tldr: Two words that get used interchangeably but mean very different things for your career and your contract.
docs.body:
Two words that get used interchangeably but mean very different things for your career and your contract. Transition If you've spent any time in a military unit, you've heard both "lateral move" and "reclass" thrown around. They're not the same thing. Using them interchangeably will get you bad advice.

## Lateral move

A lateral move is a shift from one MOS to another **within the same occupational field** or **via an existing qualification pipeline**.

Examples:
- A 0311 who passes the Scout Sniper Basic Course and picks up 0317 as a secondary MOS.
- A comms Marine who shifts between 0621 and 0627 based on unit needs and additional training.
- An Army 11B who becomes an 11C (Indirect Fire Infantryman) through internal unit assignment and additional training.

Lateral moves are usually:
- Approved at the unit or community-manager level.
- Don't require re-enlistment.
- Keep you on your current contract.
- Happen faster (months, not years).

## Reclassification (reclass)

A reclass is a **formal MOS change**, typically out of your current occupational field entirely.

Examples:
- A 0311 rifleman who re-enlists and reclassifies to 0231 Intelligence Specialist.
- An Army 25B Information Technology Specialist who reclassifies to 35F All-Source Intelligence Analyst.
- A Navy BM who reclassifies to a technical rating like ET (Electronics Technician).

Reclasses are usually:
- Approved at the service-level classification board.
- Gated on re-enlistment or a specific window in your contract.
- Require you to meet the ASVAB line scores for the new MOS.
- Subject to "needs of the service" — if the new MOS isn't under-strength, your package may not get approved.
- Involve going to a new school from scratch.

## Why this matters

If someone tells you to "just lateral over" to a different MOS, and that MOS is in a different occupational field, they're using the wrong word and you're going to end up disappointed.

The honest version: if you want to stay in the same field, a lateral move might work. If you want to leave the field entirely, you're looking at a reclass, which means re-enlistment, paperwork, approval at a higher level, and possibly a wait.

## What to do about it

1. **Identify the target MOS.** Get the code, not a nickname.
2. **Check your ASVAB line scores.** If you don't meet the composite minimum for the target MOS, you can't reclass into it regardless of everything else. You can retake the ASVAB while on active duty if needed.
3. **Ask your classification rep** — not your buddy, not a recruiter who hasn't seen you in three years. Your admin shop or career counselor is the source of truth for what's currently authorized.
4. **Time it right.** Most services only process reclass packages during a re-enlistment window.

No one's going to walk you through this if you don't ask. Start asking twelve months before your EAS.
